source: Derived from real events recorded in DECISIONS_LOG.md (2026-07-01 session). Every lesson below cites the actual incident it came from — nothing here is invented.

core_philosophy.statement: Ground truth over narrative confidence. A claim about this project's architecture, features, or state — no matter which AI assistant produced it, no matter how confident or detailed it sounds — is only as good as its citation to the real repository (source code, migrations, git log, deployed behavior). When a claim and the repo disagree, the repo wins.
core_philosophy.one_line: Verify against the repo. Every time. No exception for confidence or detail.
applies_to: any future AI assistant or human contributor

key_lessons.title: Verify scope against the real repo before implementing pasted content
key_lessons.statement: Before implementing any specification pasted from another AI conversation, grep the actual repository for the classes, tables, and env vars it claims exist. If they don't exist, that's a signal to pause and confirm scope with the human — not to start building on top of a hallucinated foundation.
key_lessons.why_it_matters: Twice in one session, content attributed to another AI assistant described an entirely different product (a Neo4j graph database; a Stripe/USD/PCI-DSS cross-border escrow platform) with zero grounding in this repository. Both were caught before a single line was written by checking the real codebase first, and both were confirmed rejected by the project owner.
evidence: DECISIONS_LOG.md — 2026-07-01 — Rejected: cross-border USD/Stripe/PCI-DSS escrow platform
checklist: grep the repo for the specific classes/tables/endpoints the new content references
if they don't exist, ask whether this is a real pivot or unrelated content before writing code
never let confidence or level of detail in a pasted spec substitute for checking it against the repo

key_lessons.title: A single source of truth must be derived from code, never hand-typed
key_lessons.statement: Any 'status' or 'context' document meant to keep multiple readers (human or AI) aligned must be generated by parsing the real repository, not hand-maintained as a static summary. A hand-typed summary can say anything and silently drift from reality.
key_lessons.why_it_matters: The original SessionStart hook (.claude/scripts/daily-briefing.sh) hardcoded a fixed status block claiming the same '35 skills' and the same 'done this session' work every single session, regardless of what was actually true. That's exactly the kind of document that causes cross-AI disagreement, because nothing forces it to match reality.
evidence: PROJECT_STATUS.md consistency checks (dead skill endpoints, missing route components, duplicate IDs) that fail CI when the registry claims something the code doesn't back up
checklist: prefer a generator script over a hand-maintained doc for anything claimed to be 'current state'
make the generator fail loudly (non-zero exit) when it finds a real inconsistency
wire the generator into CI so drift is caught before merge, not discovered later

key_lessons.title: Fairness requires hearing and notifying both sides before deciding
key_lessons.statement: Any process that adjudicates between two parties (a dispute, an approval, an escalation) must give both sides visibility and a chance to respond before a decision is finalized — not just the side that has the microphone or triggered the workflow.
key_lessons.why_it_matters: The first version of the order-dispute feature only emailed the producer and the system owner. The buyer who opened a dispute — or the producer being disputed against — had no visibility into their own case and no way to respond before an admin decided.
evidence: backend/disputes.js respond() — the non-opening party can submit counter-evidence before resolution
backend/disputes.js track() — either party can check status independently
sendDisputeNotification() now emails both the producer and the buyer at every stage, not just the producer
checklist: identify both parties in any adjudication flow, not just the one who initiated it
give the non-initiating party a documented way to respond before the decision is final
notify all parties at open/respond/resolve, not just at the end
